description: 10/10 autonomous school time-planner: liquid-glass macOS app that discovers deadlines from codex-mem, ranks by urgency, quizzes on weak subjects, and syncs blocks to iPhone calendar.

userStories.title: Fix glassmorphic transparency and visual foundation
userStories.description: The current UI is opaque and does not use proper macOS vibrancy. Fix NSVisualEffectView configuration, set window background to clear, ensure all panels use `.hudWindow` or `.sidebar` material, and remove all hardcoded gray backgrounds that kill the glass effect.
acceptanceCriteria: Main window background is transparent/vibrancy-backed — no solid gray or black fill visible behind panels
All three columns use NSVisualEffectView with appropriate material (sidebar left, fullScreenUI center, hudWindow right)
Window title bar is hidden or uses unified style — clean macOS 15 look
TimedCard uses translucent fill (Color(.windowBackgroundColor).opacity(0.45)) not a solid Color(.controlBackgroundColor)
App builds and launches without visual regressions
userStories.notes: The key fix is in VisualEffectView.swift and ContentView.swift. The window must be set to .titled | .fullSizeContentView and backgroundColor = .clear. NSHostingView background should also be cleared. In ContentView, replace any Color(.controlBackground) fills with Color.clear or a very low-opacity white. The TimedCard component in TimedCard.swift should use `.ultraThinMaterial` background. Check that all ZStack/VStack backgrounds are not accidentally opaque. In TimeManagerDesktopApp.swift, configure the WindowGroup with .windowStyle(.hiddenTitleBar) or equivalent.

userStories.title: Redesign 3-pane layout for intuitive flow
userStories.description: The current layout is a shitshow with repetitive sections and unclear hierarchy. Redesign to: LEFT=Task Library (compact, icon+title only, expandable), CENTRE=Command Surface (ranked tasks + prompt composer + today's schedule stacked vertically, clear section headers), RIGHT=Context Drawer (collapsible, only shows when a task is selected or quiz is active). Reduce visual noise by 60%.
acceptanceCriteria: Left column is max 220px, shows task list as compact rows (icon, title, urgency dot, due date) — no expanded detail cards in left panel
Center column has three clear sections: 'Next Up' (top 3 ranked tasks with action buttons), 'Today's Plan' (schedule blocks), 'Command' (prompt input at bottom) — each with a small section header
Right panel is hidden by default, slides in when user selects a task or clicks 'Context' — shows task detail + relevant context snippets
No repeated task information across columns — each piece of data appears exactly once
All column widths have sensible min/max constraints (left: 180–260, right: 260–360)
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: This is a major ContentView.swift restructure. Key changes: (1) Left panel becomes a flat list — remove the nested VStack card per task, use a plain List with compact row height. (2) Center panel uses a VStack with three named sections — pin 'Next Up' to top, 'Today's Plan' scrollable middle, 'Command' pinned to bottom. (3) Right panel uses a slide-in animation driven by @State selectedTask: PlannerTask?. When selectedTask != nil, show task detail + context. (4) Remove all the duplicate import/context panels that currently appear in both columns. (5) Section headers should be 11px uppercase tracking-wide labels. (6) Use a thin 1px Divider() between sections, not padding.

userStories.title: CodexMem deadline discovery — auto-load school subjects and deadlines
userStories.description: On launch, Timed must query codex-mem (via the MCP server at localhost or via shell `codex-mem search`) to discover Ammar's upcoming deadlines. It must find: Economics test (Friday, most important), English (second), Maths investigation (Wednesday, third), Society and Culture photo essay (last). Surface these as pre-seeded tasks with correct importance scores if they do not already exist.
acceptanceCriteria: On app launch (or on first load of PlannerStore), CodexMemorySchoolPack.swift queries codex-mem for deadline-related memories
Search terms used include: 'economics test', 'english', 'maths investigation', 'society and culture', 'deadline', 'due', 'assessment'
If Economics test found in memory: task created/updated with importance=10, deadline=next Friday
If English found: importance=8
If Maths investigation found: importance=7, deadline=next Wednesday
If Society and Culture found: importance=5
Tasks created from codex-mem have source='codex-mem' and are flagged as auto-discovered
PlannerStore logs which memories were found at startup — visible in console
App builds and the discovery runs without crashing
userStories.notes: CodexMemorySchoolPack.swift already exists. Extend it with a `discoverDeadlines()` async method. The codex-mem MCP server exposes a search tool. Check if there is a CLI: `codex-mem search <query>` or if it's accessed via the MCP protocol. If CLI: use Process() to call it and parse JSON output. If MCP only: check if there is a REST shim at http://localhost:PORT. The method should run 4 searches (one per subject), parse results, and call back into PlannerStore to upsert tasks. Use `PlannerStore.upsertTaskHints()` or create a new `applyDiscoveredDeadlines([DiscoveredDeadline])` method. Importance mapping: Economics=10, English=8, Maths=7, SoC=5. Deadline mapping: Economics=next Friday (calculate from today), Maths=next Wednesday, English/SoC=no hard deadline unless found in memory. Call `discoverDeadlines()` from PlannerStore.init() via Task { await ... }.

userStories.title: Planning engine — wire codex-mem scores into urgency ranking
userStories.description: PlanningEngine.rank() currently uses static weights. Extend it to consume the codex-mem confidence scores and subject importance discovered in STORY-003, ensuring Economics always floats to #1, Maths before Wednesday floats to #2-3, English stays prominent, and SoC ranks last of the four.
acceptanceCriteria: PlanningEngine accepts a `subjectImportanceMap: [String: Double]` parameter (or reads it from PlannerStore)
When ranking, a task's subject importance score multiplies the base importance weight (not just adds)
Economics test ranks #1 in all scenarios where it is incomplete and deadline is within 7 days
Maths investigation ranks above English when deadline is within 3 days
Subject confidence from codex-mem (if found) is used to set confidenceGap — lower confidence = higher urgency
A debug method `PlanningEngine.explainRank(task:)` returns a string showing score breakdown — useful for testing
App builds, PlanningEngine tests pass (or are added)
userStories.notes: Edit PlanningEngine.swift. Current signature: `func rank(_ tasks: [PlannerTask], promptBoost: String?) -> [RankedTask]`. Add `subjectImportance: [String: Double] = [:]` param. In the scoring loop, add: `let subjectMult = subjectImportance[task.subject ?? ""] ?? 1.0` and apply it as a multiplier to the importance component. Also add a `confidenceOverride: [String: Double] = [:]` param — if the task's subject has a codex-mem confidence override, use that instead of task.confidence. Write a simple `explainRank(task:context:) -> String` that returns 'importance=X*mult=Y deadline=Z total=N'. Add unit tests in Tests/ that verify Economics ranks above English ranks above SoC given identical deadline proximity.

userStories.title: Seqta sync — real deadline extraction and subject matching
userStories.description: SeqtaBackgroundSync.swift currently imports tasks but does not reliably extract due dates or match to the correct subject. Fix due date parsing, improve subject keyword matching, and ensure Seqta tasks appear with correct deadline and subject fields in the planner.
acceptanceCriteria: Seqta tasks with a due date string (e.g. '26 March 2026', '26/03/2026', 'Due: Monday') are parsed into a Date and stored in task.deadline

Subject matching maps 'Economics', 'Eco', 'SACE Economics' → Economics; 'English', 'Eng Stds' → English; 'Mathematical Methods', 'Maths', 'Specialist' → Maths; 'Society and Culture', 'SoC', 'S&C' → Society and Culture
Tasks imported from Seqta show the correct subject label in the UI
Re-importing the same Seqta data does not create duplicate tasks (idempotent by stable ID)
A seqta import log entry is written to console showing: tasks found, tasks created, tasks updated, tasks skipped
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Edit SeqtaBackgroundSync.swift and ImportPipeline.swift. For date parsing: try multiple formats using DateFormatter — 'd MMMM yyyy', 'd/MM/yyyy', 'EEE d MMM'. Also handle relative: 'today', 'tomorrow', 'Monday' (use Calendar.current.nextWeekday()). For subject matching: build a dictionary in SubjectCatalog.swift (or Models.swift) mapping keyword patterns to canonical subject names. Use case-insensitive prefix/contains matching. For deduplication: stable ID = seqta task ID or SHA256(title + subject + source). Check ImportPipeline for existing dedup logic and extend it.

userStories.title: Obsidian vault context — deep import and subject-tagged retrieval
userStories.description: ObsidianVaultImporter.swift exists but is shallow. Extend it to recursively scan the vault, extract note content per subject folder, and make that content available in the context panel when the matching subject is selected.
acceptanceCriteria: Vault path is configurable in Preferences.swift (defaulting to ~/Documents/Obsidian or prompting user on first run)
Importer scans all .md files recursively and indexes them by subject based on folder name or #tag
Subject matching uses the same canonical map as STORY-005
When a subject is selected in the right panel (or quiz is active for a subject), the top 3 most relevant Obsidian notes for that subject are shown as expandable snippets
Note content is truncated to first 400 chars in the snippet, with a 'Show more' expansion
Import runs in background on app launch and on demand via a 'Sync Vault' button in Settings
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: ObsidianVaultImporter.swift needs: (1) recursive file enumeration using FileManager.default.enumerator(at:includingPropertiesForKeys:); (2) a ContextDocument struct with { id, subject, title, content, path, importedAt }; (3) store as [ContextDocument] in PlannerStore.obsidianDocuments; (4) a `topDocuments(for subject: String, limit: Int) -> [ContextDocument]` method using simple keyword frequency scoring; (5) in ContentView, in the right panel (task detail / context drawer), add an 'Obsidian Notes' section that calls topDocuments for the selected task's subject. Store vault path in Preferences with key 'obsidianVaultPath'.

userStories.title: Quiz mode overhaul — intuitive study interface with real content
userStories.description: Current quiz mode is a plain chat. Replace it with a dedicated full-screen study overlay: shows subject, shows a generated question (from actual Obsidian/transcript context), waits for answer, gives immediate feedback with correct answer and source note. Track session stats (questions attempted, correct, confidence delta).
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Study' button per subject task opens a full-screen modal (not a sheet) with dark glassmorphic overlay
First screen: subject name, confidence score, 'Start Quiz' button
Questions are generated by sending context (top 3 Obsidian notes + any transcript content) to Codex with a quiz generation prompt — minimum 5 questions per session
Each question shows: question text, 4 multiple-choice options (A/B/C/D), a text field for free-form answer, and 'Submit' button
On submit: show correct answer highlighted green, incorrect answer highlighted red, 1-2 sentence explanation, source note title
After 5+ questions: show session summary — X/Y correct, confidence delta (+N%), 'End Session' or 'Keep Going' buttons
On 'End Session': update task.confidence in PlannerStore, trigger rebuildPlan()
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create a new QuizView.swift (or extend the existing quiz UI in ContentView). Key components: (1) QuizSessionModel: @Observable class holding currentQuestion, questionHistory, sessionScore, subject. (2) QuizQuestionView: shows question + 4 options as large tappable cards. (3) Question generation: call CodexBridge with a prompt like 'Generate 5 quiz questions from this content: [context]. Return JSON array of {question, options: [A,B,C,D], correct: A, explanation, source}. (4) Parse JSON response to [QuizQuestion] model. (5) Track correct answers, calculate confidence delta: delta = (correct/total - 0.5) * 20 clamped to -10..+10. (6) After session, call store.applySubjectConfidences([subject: newConfidence]). The modal should use .fullScreenCover or a ZStack overlay with blur background.

userStories.title: Focus timer — inline Pomodoro with task binding
userStories.description: Add a focus timer that binds to a specific task. When started, a small timer widget appears in the center column above the command composer. Timer counts down (25min default, configurable). On completion: plays a sound, marks a pomodoro on the task, prompts for a 5-min break. After 4 pomodoros: long break prompt.
acceptanceCriteria: Each task row in the ranked list has a small ▶ play button — clicking it starts a focus session bound to that task
A timer widget appears at the top of the center column showing: task title (truncated), time remaining (MM:SS), a thin progress ring, pause/stop buttons
Timer duration is configurable in Settings (default 25min, options: 15, 25, 45, 60)
On timer completion: NSSound plays a soft chime (use NSSound.beep() or a bundled .mp3), a notification is posted via UNUserNotificationCenter
Completed pomodoro is recorded on the task (task.pomodoroCount += 1)
After 4 pomodoros on any task: show 'Take a long break (15 min)?' prompt as a banner
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create FocusTimerModel.swift: @Observable class with currentTask: PlannerTask?, secondsRemaining: Int, isRunning: Bool, pomodoroCount: Int. Use Timer.scheduledTimer or Swift Concurrency (Task { while isRunning { try await Task.sleep(nanoseconds: 1_000_000_000); secondsRemaining -= 1 } }). FocusTimerWidget.swift: a compact HStack showing task name, circular progress (drawn with a SwiftUI Circle stroke with trim()), and controls. Inject FocusTimerModel as @State in ContentView and pass it down. For notifications: request UNUserNotificationCenter.current().requestAuthorization(options: [.alert, .sound]) on first launch and trigger a UNMutableNotificationContent when timer hits 0. Store pomodoroCount in PlannerTask model (add field to Models.swift and PlannerSnapshot).

userStories.title: Deadline alert system — menubar badge and notification center
userStories.description: Add a background deadline monitor that posts notification center alerts when a deadline is within 24h, 4h, and 1h. Also show a red badge on the app's Dock icon when any task deadline is within 24h. Show an inline deadline countdown in the task row for tasks due within 48h.
acceptanceCriteria: App requests notification permission on first launch
For each incomplete task with a deadline: schedule UNUserNotificationCenter notifications at deadline-24h, deadline-4h, deadline-1h
Notifications fire even when app is in background (use UNTimeIntervalNotificationTrigger or UNCalendarNotificationTrigger)
Dock icon badge shows count of tasks due within 24h using NSApp.dockTile.badgeLabel
Task rows with deadline within 48h show a compact countdown badge: '23h', '4h 30m', 'Overdue' in red
Notifications are re-scheduled on every app launch and when a task's deadline changes
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create DeadlineAlertService.swift with a `scheduleAlerts(for tasks: [PlannerTask])` method. Use UNUserNotificationCenter: for each task with deadline != nil && !task.completed, remove existing notifications (removeDeliveredNotifications + removePendingNotificationRequests with task-based IDs), then add three new requests at t-24h, t-4h, t-1h. In PlannerStore, call scheduleAlerts whenever tasks change (in save() or a didSet observer). For Dock badge: `NSApp.dockTile.badgeLabel = count > 0 ? "\(count)" : nil`. For the countdown display in task rows: compute `timeUntilDeadline` as a formatted string using `RelativeDateTimeFormatter` or manual calculation. Countdown badge: use a Text with foreground color .red and a small capsule background.

userStories.title: Subject study pane — dedicated study mode with progress tracking
userStories.description: Add a 'Study Mode' toggle in the app toolbar that replaces the three-column layout with a focused two-column study view: LEFT=subject list with confidence bars, RIGHT=study surface (quiz + notes + context for selected subject). This is a completely separate screen optimised for active revision sessions.
acceptanceCriteria: A 'Study Mode' toolbar button (book icon) in the main toolbar switches the entire window to study mode
Study mode left panel: list of all subjects with a confidence bar (0-100%) beneath each, sorted by lowest confidence first
Clicking a subject in study mode loads the right panel with: subject name + confidence %, top 3 Obsidian notes, any relevant transcript excerpts, and a 'Start Quiz' button
Notes and excerpts are searchable within study mode using a search field at the top of the right panel
Confidence bars update in real time after a quiz session completes
A 'Back to Planner' button in the toolbar returns to the normal 3-pane layout
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create StudyModeView.swift as a top-level View. Add @State isStudyMode: Bool to ContentView (or to PlannerStore). When isStudyMode is true, the main body shows StudyModeView instead of the normal HSplitView. StudyModeView: left panel is a List of SubjectStudyRow (subject name + ProgressView(value: confidence/100)); right panel is SubjectStudyDetail (notes, excerpts, quiz button). Use the Obsidian docs from STORY-006 and ContextDocument list. Subject list is derived from: Set of all task subjects + all ContextDocument subjects. Confidence values come from task.confidence or a dedicated subjectConfidences: [String: Double] dictionary in PlannerStore (add this field). Sync subjectConfidences after quiz sessions.

userStories.title: Full-text context search across all imported content
userStories.description: Add a search bar in the right panel (or as a command palette) that searches across all imported content: Seqta tasks, Obsidian notes, transcript excerpts, codex-mem hits, and chat history. Returns ranked results with source labels.
acceptanceCriteria: A search field (⌘K keyboard shortcut to focus) is accessible from the main layout — either in the toolbar or at the top of the right panel
Typing triggers real-time search across: PlannerTask titles/descriptions, ContextDocument content, transcript excerpts, and chat messages
Results are grouped by source type (Tasks, Notes, Transcripts, Chat)
Each result shows: title, source type icon, subject badge, first matching sentence highlighted
Clicking a result: if task → select task and show in right panel; if note → open note preview in right panel; if chat message → scroll chat to that message
Search is client-side (no network) and handles up to 10,000 documents without lag
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create a ContextSearchService.swift with: `func search(_ query: String, in store: PlannerStore) -> [SearchResult]`. SearchResult: { id, type: .task/.note/.transcript/.chat, title, subject, snippet, relevanceScore }. Scoring: exact title match = 100, substring match in title = 80, content match = 50, weighted by recency. For performance: build an in-memory index at startup — Dict<String, [SearchResult]> keyed by first 3 chars of each word. For the UI: create SearchPanelView.swift with a focused TextField, results List grouped by type. Wire ⌘K using .keyboardShortcut('k', modifiers: .command) on a hidden Button.

userStories.title: Confidence calibration interview — onboarding quiz for new subjects
userStories.description: When a new subject appears (via Seqta import or manual add) with no confidence data, automatically trigger a 5-question calibration quiz. This asks Ammar how well he knows the subject, what topics he's covered, and what he's unsure about. Uses his answers to set initial confidence and generate study hints.
acceptanceCriteria: When a task is added with a subject that has no existing confidence score, a banner appears: 'New subject: Economics. Run a quick confidence check?' with 'Yes, 2 min' and 'Skip' buttons

On 'Yes': opens a compact popover (not full screen) with 5 quick self-assessment questions: 'How confident are you in this topic? (1-5)', 'What's the most recent thing you covered?', 'What are you least sure about?', 'When is the assessment?', 'What resources do you have?'
Answers are sent to Codex: it responds with an initial confidence score (0-100) and 3 specific study hints for that subject
Study hints are stored as task.notes and surfaced in the context panel
Initial confidence score is applied to the subject in subjectConfidences
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create ConfidenceCalibrationView.swift as a popover or sheet. Trigger: in PlannerStore.importCurrentPayload() (or upsertTaskHints()), after inserting a task, check if subjectConfidences[task.subject] == nil. If so, set a @Published pendingCalibrationSubject: String? property. In ContentView, show a banner when pendingCalibrationSubject != nil. The popover has 5 simple form fields. On submit, call CodexBridge with prompt: 'Given these self-assessment answers about [subject]: [answers], return a JSON object with { confidence: Int (0-100), hints: [String] }. Be realistic and slightly pessimistic.' Parse response and apply.

userStories.title: Daily overview card — morning briefing and day plan summary
userStories.description: Add a 'Today' card at the top of the center column (above the ranked task list) that shows: date, top 3 priorities for today, total scheduled hours, any deadlines today/tomorrow, and a one-line motivational planning prompt. Generated fresh each app launch.
acceptanceCriteria: A compact card appears at the very top of the center column, above the ranked task list

Card shows: today's date (styled as 'Thursday, 26 March'), top 3 task titles with urgency color dots, total hours planned today (sum of schedule blocks), any tasks due today or tomorrow in a red/amber badge
A 'Generate Day Plan' button triggers a Codex call that returns a 2-sentence day plan summary and injects it as a pinned message in the main chat
The card is collapsible (chevron toggle) and remembers its collapsed state
Card background uses a subtle gradient from the primary subject color (Economics = warm gold, English = blue, Maths = purple, SoC = green)
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: Create DailyOverviewCard.swift. Data it needs: store.tasks (for top 3 ranked), store.scheduleBlocks.filter { Calendar.current.isDateInToday($0.start) } for total hours, tasks with deadline in next 2 days. Place it in the center column VStack before the ranked list. 'Generate Day Plan' calls CodexBridge with a prompt built from today's top tasks + schedule. Subject color map: Economics = Color(hex: '#B8860B'), English = .blue, Maths = .purple, SoC = .green — use the top-ranked task's subject to tint the card. Persist isCollapsed in UserDefaults.

userStories.title: Keyboard-first navigation and command palette
userStories.description: Add full keyboard navigation: ↑/↓ to select tasks in the ranked list, Enter to open detail, ⌘N to add task, ⌘S to start study mode, ⌘T to start focus timer on selected task, ⌘E to export calendar, ⌘/ to show keyboard shortcut reference. Make the app usable without touching the mouse.
acceptanceCriteria: Arrow keys navigate the ranked task list (focused task is highlighted with a selection ring, not just a hover)
Enter on a focused task opens the right panel with task detail
⌘N opens the AddTaskSheet
⌘S toggles study mode (STORY-010)
⌘T starts focus timer on the currently selected task
⌘E triggers calendar export
⌘/ or ? opens a KeyboardShortcutsHelpView popover listing all shortcuts
⌘K focuses the search field (STORY-011)
Tab cycles between left/center/right panels
App builds without errors
userStories.notes: In ContentView.swift, use .focusedValue and @FocusState for the ranked task list. For list navigation: maintain @State selectedTaskIndex: Int and use onKeyPress(.upArrow) / onKeyPress(.downArrow) (macOS 14+). For ⌘N, ⌘S etc: use .keyboardShortcut modifiers on hidden Buttons or use NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents. Create KeyboardShortcutsHelpView.swift: a simple grid of shortcut + description pairs in a popover. The most important shortcut is ⌘K for search — make that the first thing that works.

userStories.title: Polish pass — animations, empty states, error handling, and app icon
userStories.description: Final polish: add meaningful animations to state transitions, handle all empty states gracefully, show proper error messages when Codex/import fails, and set a proper app icon. The app must feel finished, not prototypal.
acceptanceCriteria: All list insertions/removals use .animation(.spring(response: 0.3)) transitions

Empty states: when task list is empty show an illustration + 'Add your first task with ⌘N'; when no schedule blocks show 'Ask Timed to plan your day'; when context panel is empty show 'Select a task to see context'
When CodexBridge fails (non-zero exit, empty response), show an inline error banner in the chat area with the error message and a 'Retry' button — do not silently fail
When Seqta sync fails, show a Settings badge and a toast notification
App icon is set to the TimedLogoMark rendered at all required sizes (16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024) and added to Assets.xcassets/AppIcon.appiconset
swift build completes with zero errors and zero warnings (fix all existing warnings)
App launches in under 2 seconds on first cold start
userStories.notes: For animations: add .transition(.asymmetric(insertion: .scale.combined(with: .opacity), removal: .opacity)) to task rows and schedule blocks. For empty states: create EmptyStateView.swift with icon + title + subtitle + optional action button. For error handling in CodexBridge: capture stderr separately, surface it to PlannerStore as a @Published lastError: String?, show as a dismissable banner. For app icon: in TimedLogoMark.swift there is already a logo render — export it using ImageRenderer at all required sizes and write PNG files to Assets.xcassets/AppIcon.appiconset/ along with a Contents.json. Fix all Swift warnings (unused vars, deprecated APIs). Run swift build and resolve every diagnostic.

userStories.title: Menu bar live Pomodoro countdown
userStories.description: Add an NSStatusItem in the macOS menu bar that shows the active Pomodoro countdown (e.g. '🍅 18:42') in real time. Clicking it shows a mini popover with pause/stop controls and the current task title. When no timer is running, show the hourglass icon only.
acceptanceCriteria: NSStatusItem appears in menu bar whenever Timed is running
While timer is active, title updates every second showing MM:SS remaining
Clicking the status item opens a compact popover: task title, time remaining, pause/resume button, stop button
When timer is not running, status item shows '⏳' with no countdown
On break, shows '☕ 04:30' style label
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Create MenuBarController.swift. Use NSStatusBar.system.statusItem(withLength: NSStatusItem.variableLength). Observe FocusTimerModel via @MainActor. Use a Timer.scheduledTimer on main runloop to update the button title every second. Popover: NSPopover containing a small SwiftUI view wrapping FocusTimerWidget. Instantiate MenuBarController in TimeManagerDesktopApp and keep a strong reference.

userStories.title: Spaced repetition engine for quiz cards (SM-2)
userStories.description: Replace the random quiz question picker with SM-2 spaced repetition. Each QuizQuestion gets an ease factor, interval (days), and next-due date stored in PlannerStore. The quiz surfaces only cards due today. After answering, update ease/interval based on quality score 0-5. Confidence scores update automatically post-session.
acceptanceCriteria: QuizQuestion model gains fields: `easeFactor: Double`, `interval: Int`, `nextDue: Date`, persisted to JSON
PlannerStore.dueQuizCards(for subject:) returns only cards where nextDue <= today
QuizView shows due-today count badge per subject in StudyModeView
After each answer, SM-2 update runs: quality 5=perfect, 3=correct, 1=wrong — updates easeFactor and interval
On session end, confidence score for subject auto-updates to average quality / 5.0
If no cards are due, QuizView shows 'All caught up — next review in Xd' message
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add SM2 logic in a new SpacedRepetitionEngine.swift. SM-2 formula: if quality >= 3: interval = (interval==1 ? 6 : round(interval * easeFactor)); easeFactor = max(1.3, easeFactor + 0.1 - (5-q)*(0.08+(5-q)*0.02)); else: interval=1. Store nextDue as ISO8601 string. PlannerStore.dueQuizCards filters by nextDue date. QuizView needs a 'rate your recall' step after showing answer (buttons: Again / Hard / Good / Easy mapping to q=1/2/4/5).

userStories.title: Pomodoro statistics — daily counts, streaks, and 7-day sparkline
userStories.description: Track how many Pomodoros are completed each day. Store a `[String: Int]` date→count log in PlannerStore (persisted to JSON). DailyOverviewCard shows: today's pomodoro count, current study streak (consecutive days with ≥1 pomodoro), and a 7-bar mini sparkline using SwiftUI Charts.
acceptanceCriteria: PlannerStore has `pomodoroLog: [String: Int]` (key = 'yyyy-MM-dd') persisted to the save file
Every time FocusTimerModel fires onTaskCompletion, PlannerStore increments today's log entry
DailyOverviewCard shows '🍅 3 today' label
DailyOverviewCard shows '🔥 5 day streak' (or 0 if none)
A 7-bar Chart (SwiftUI Charts) shows last 7 days of pomodoro counts below the overview text
swift build passes, Charts framework imported correctly
userStories.notes: Import Charts framework (available in macOS 13+). Add `pomodoroLog: [String: Int]` to PlannerStore, include in Codable save. Add `recordPomodoro()` func that bumps today's key. Wire it into the onTaskCompletion callback in ContentView where FocusTimerModel.start() is called. Streak = count backwards from today while log[date] > 0. In DailyOverviewCard.swift, add a Chart { ForEach(last7Days) { BarMark(...) } } block under the existing content.

userStories.title: Natural language task input parser
userStories.description: The ⌘N 'Add task' field should accept natural language like 'Biology essay due Friday importance 8' or 'Maths test tomorrow' and auto-parse into TaskItem fields (title, dueDate, importance, subject) using a local regex/keyword parser — no AI needed. Unparsed tokens become the title.
acceptanceCriteria: Input 'Economics test due Friday importance 10' creates a task with dueDate=next Friday, importance=10, subject guessed as 'Economics'
Input 'Maths homework tomorrow' creates task with dueDate=tomorrow, subject='Maths'
Input 'Read chapter 3' creates task with title 'Read chapter 3', no date, importance=5 default
Parsed fields shown as inline chips below the input before confirming (e.g. '📅 Fri', '⭐ 10', '📚 Economics')
Parser handles: due/by/on + (today|tomorrow|monday..sunday|next week), importance/priority + digit, subject keywords matching existing subjects
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Create NLTaskParser.swift with a `parse(_ input: String) -> ParsedTask` struct. Use NSDataDetector for date extraction OR a manual regex: `(due|by|on)\s+(today|tomorrow|monday|tuesday|wednesday|thursday|friday|saturday|sunday|next week)`. For importance: `(importance|priority|imp)\s*(\d+)`. For subject: check input.lowercased() against known subjects ['economics','english','maths','biology','society','history','physics','chemistry']. Return a ParsedTask with optional fields. In ContentView, show chip previews in the add-task sheet using HStack of small rounded Text badges.

userStories.title: Task notes with inline Markdown rendering
userStories.description: Each TaskItem gets a `notes: String` field. In the right context drawer, show the notes as rendered Markdown (bold, italics, bullet lists, code blocks). ⌘E toggles between edit (plain TextEditor) and preview (AttributedString rendered Text). Notes persist with the task.
acceptanceCriteria: TaskItem model has `notes: String` field, default empty, included in Codable
Right context drawer shows a notes section below task details
Preview mode renders markdown via AttributedString(markdown:) — bold, italic, bullets, inline code all render
⌘E or a pencil button toggles edit/preview mode
Changes auto-save on every keystroke (debounced 500ms)
Notes field shows placeholder text 'Add notes…' when empty in edit mode
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add `notes: String = ''` to TaskItem. In the right context drawer section of ContentView.swift (or a new TaskDetailView.swift), add a two-mode notes component. Use @State isEditingNotes: Bool. Preview: `Text(try! AttributedString(markdown: notes))`. Edit: `TextEditor(text: $task.notes)` with .font(.body). ⌘E KeyboardShortcut to toggle. Debounce save with a .onChange modifier + DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(0.5) pattern.

userStories.title: Subject confidence trend chart (7-day history)
userStories.description: ConfidenceCalibrationView saves a timestamped history of confidence readings per subject. StudyModeView shows a SwiftUI Charts line chart of the last 7 readings per subject, so Ammar can see if his confidence is trending up or down.
acceptanceCriteria: PlannerStore has `confidenceHistory: [String: [ConfidenceReading]]` where ConfidenceReading has date: Date and value: Double
Every time calibration is submitted, a new ConfidenceReading is appended (capped at 30 entries per subject)
StudyModeView subject detail panel shows a LineChart of last 7 readings using SwiftUI Charts
Chart x-axis shows abbreviated dates (Mon, Tue…), y-axis 0-100
If fewer than 2 readings exist, show 'Complete calibration to see trend' placeholder
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add ConfidenceReading: Codable { let date: Date; let value: Double } to Models.swift. Add `confidenceHistory: [String: [ConfidenceReading]]` to PlannerStore, persisted. In ConfidenceCalibrationView submit handler, call `store.recordConfidence(subject:, value:)`. In StudyModeView subject detail, add Chart { ForEach(readings) { LineMark(x: .value('Date', r.date), y: .value('Score', r.value * 100)) } }.

userStories.title: AI task decomposition — break hard tasks into subtasks via Codex
userStories.description: TaskItem gets a 'Break it down' button in the context drawer. Tapping it sends the task title + subject to Codex (via CodexBridge) with a prompt asking for 3-5 concrete subtasks. The response is parsed and the subtasks added as child TaskItems with `parentId` set.
acceptanceCriteria: TaskItem model has optional `parentId: UUID?` field
Context drawer shows 'Break it down ⚡' button for tasks with no existing children
Clicking it calls CodexBridge with prompt: 'Break this task into 3-5 concrete subtasks. Task: [title]. Subject: [subject]. Return as a numbered list, one per line, no preamble.'
Response is parsed line-by-line into TaskItem objects with parentId = original task id
Subtasks appear indented under parent in the left task list
Subtask creation is undoable (⌘Z removes the batch)
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add `parentId: UUID?` to TaskItem. PlannerStore.children(of taskId:) filters tasks by parentId. In ContentView left panel, group subtasks under parent using outline-style indented rows. The 'Break it down' button in right drawer calls an async func decompose(task:) in PlannerStore that uses CodexBridge.send(prompt:) and parses numbered lines 1. ... 2. ... stripping leading number+dot. Undo: store a snapshot before adding and pop on ⌘Z.

userStories.title: Weekly review sheet — every Sunday, summarise the week
userStories.description: Every Sunday when Timed launches, show a modal 'Weekly Review' sheet. It summarises: tasks completed this week, total Pomodoros, average confidence change per subject, and the top 3 priorities for the coming week (from PlanningEngine). Dismissible and skippable.
acceptanceCriteria: PlannerStore detects first launch on or after Sunday (stores last review date)
Weekly review sheet appears as a .sheet() modal on the main window
Shows: completed task count for the past 7 days, total Pomodoros (from pomodoroLog), per-subject confidence delta (latest - 7-days-ago from confidenceHistory)
Shows top 3 ranked tasks from PlanningEngine for the coming week
Dismiss button saves today as lastReviewDate so it doesn't re-show until next Sunday
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add `lastWeeklyReview: Date?` to PlannerStore persisted state. On init, if today is Sunday and (lastWeeklyReview == nil or lastWeeklyReview < last Sunday): set showWeeklyReview = true. Create WeeklyReviewView.swift as a SwiftUI sheet. Completed tasks: filter tasks where completedDate is within last 7 days. Confidence delta: for each subject, compare confidenceHistory last entry vs entry 7 days ago. Top 3 priorities: call PlanningEngine.rank() and take first 3.

userStories.title: Drag-and-drop task reordering with manual rank override
userStories.description: The left task list supports drag-and-drop reordering. When a user manually drags a task to a new position, that position is stored as a `manualRank: Int?` override on TaskItem. Manual rank takes priority over PlanningEngine rank until the task's deadline changes.
acceptanceCriteria: TaskItem has `manualRank: Int?` field (nullable, persisted)
Left task list uses .onMove modifier to enable drag-and-drop reordering
After drag, the moved task gets manualRank = destination index * 100
PlanningEngine.rank() checks manualRank — if set and deadline unchanged, pins the task at that position
A 'Reset order' button in the task's context menu clears manualRank and restores AI ranking
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add `manualRank: Int?` to TaskItem. In ContentView left panel List, add .onMove { from, to in store.moveTask(from: from, to: to) }. In PlannerStore.moveTask, assign manualRank = to * 100 to moved item. In PlanningEngine.rank(), pre-sort by manualRank before the urgency sort for tasks that have it set. Context menu: Button('Reset AI ranking') { task.manualRank = nil; store.save() }.

userStories.title: Export daily plan to PDF via PDFKit
userStories.description: DailyOverviewCard gets an 'Export PDF' button (⌘P). It generates a clean A4 PDF of today's plan: date header, top 3 tasks with deadlines, schedule blocks as a timeline, and any active Pomodoro session note. Saves to ~/Downloads/Timed-YYYY-MM-DD.pdf and opens in Preview.
acceptanceCriteria: ⌘P or 'Export PDF' button triggers export
PDF is A4, portrait, clean layout — date header, tasks section, schedule section
Tasks section: numbered list, title + subject + deadline + importance
Schedule section: timeline showing block start/end times and labels
File saved to ~/Downloads/Timed-YYYY-MM-DD.pdf
NSWorkspace.shared.open() opens the file in Preview after save
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Create PDFExporter.swift. Use PDFKit: create PDFDocument, add a PDFPage subclass that overrides draw(with:to:). In the draw method, use NSString.draw(at:withAttributes:) and NSBezierPath for timeline bars. Page size: CGSize(width: 595, height: 842) (A4 points). Pass in today's ranked tasks + schedule blocks. Save with pdfDocument.write(to: url). Call from ContentView on ⌘P KeyboardShortcut.

userStories.title: Inline Seqta grade display in StudyModeView
userStories.description: SeqtaBackgroundSync extracts grade/mark data from Seqta (where available) and stores as GradeEntry in PlannerStore. StudyModeView shows the latest grade and grade trend per subject alongside the confidence bar, so Ammar can see both self-assessed confidence and actual performance.
acceptanceCriteria: GradeEntry model: subject, assessmentTitle, mark, outOf, date — Codable, persisted
SeqtaBackgroundSync parses grade rows from Seqta HTML and posts to PlannerStore via updateGrades()
PlannerStore has `grades: [GradeEntry]` persisted
StudyModeView subject row shows latest grade as '14/20' badge next to confidence bar
Hovering the badge shows a popover with last 3 assessments for that subject
If no grades: shows nothing (not a dash or placeholder)
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add GradeEntry: Codable to Models.swift. Add `grades: [GradeEntry]` to PlannerStore. In SeqtaBackgroundSync, after parsing tasks, also look for grade table rows in the HTML (tr elements with mark/outOf pattern like '14 / 20' or percentage). Add `latestGrade(for subject:) -> GradeEntry?` helper. In StudyModeView subject row, show Text('\(grade.mark)/\(grade.outOf)').font(.caption).foregroundColor(.secondary) next to the confidence ProgressView.

userStories.title: Global hotkey (⌘⌥T) to start focus timer from anywhere
userStories.description: Register a global macOS hotkey ⌘⌥T using CGEventTap or NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents. When triggered from any app, Timed comes to front and immediately starts a Pomodoro for the top-ranked incomplete task. Shows a HUD-style overlay confirming the timer started.
acceptanceCriteria: Global hotkey ⌘⌥T is registered on app launch
When triggered from any app: NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true), then FocusTimerModel.start() for the #1 ranked task
A HUD overlay (NSPanel or SwiftUI overlay) shows for 2 seconds: '🍅 Started: [task title]' then fades out
Hotkey works even when Timed window is minimised or behind other windows
Hotkey is released/unregistered on app termination
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Use NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents(matching: .keyDown) to detect ⌘⌥T (keyCode 17 = T, modifiers .command | .option). Store the monitor token and remove in applicationWillTerminate. On trigger: DispatchQueue.main.async { NSApp.activate(); store.focusTimer.start(for: store.rankedTasks.first?.task, ...) }. HUD: create a small NSPanel (non-activating, HUD level) with a SwiftUI view that auto-dismisses after 2s using DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter.

userStories.title: Obsidian AI summary — 3-bullet context digest for selected task
userStories.description: When a task is selected in the context drawer and there are relevant Obsidian notes, add an 'AI Summary' section that sends the top 3 note snippets to Codex with prompt 'Summarise in 3 bullet points relevant to: [task title]'. Display the bullets as a compact digest. Cached per task.
acceptanceCriteria: Context drawer shows 'AI Summary' section below note snippets when a task is selected
On first view of section, a 'Generate' button is shown (not auto-triggered to save API calls)
Clicking Generate: calls CodexBridge with the top 3 Obsidian snippets + task title, requests 3-bullet summary
Bullets render as a compact list with '•' prefix
Summary is cached in memory keyed by task.id — does not re-generate on re-select
Shows loading spinner while generating, error banner on failure
swift build passes
userStories.notes:
Add `obsidianSummaryCache: [UUID: String]` to PlannerStore (not persisted, in-memory only). In the right context drawer, below ObsidianNoteSnippetCard, add an AISummarySection view. State: .idle / .loading / .done(String) / .error(String). Generate button calls an async func generateObsidianSummary(for task:) on PlannerStore. Prompt: 'Summarise these notes in exactly 3 bullet points relevant to the task: [title]

[snippets joined by 
---
]'. Parse response lines starting with '-' or '•' or '1.'.

userStories.title: Task completion celebration — confetti + sound on finish
userStories.description: When a task is marked complete (checkbox ticked or swipe-to-complete), show a brief confetti burst animation over the task row and play a soft chime sound. Uses SpriteKit particle system or a pure SwiftUI particle effect. Duration: 1.2 seconds. No confetti for subtasks.
acceptanceCriteria: Marking a top-level task complete triggers a confetti burst anchored to the task row
Confetti uses 3-4 colours matching the glassmorphic palette (white, gold, light blue, green)
A short chime sound plays (NSSound or AudioServicesPlaySystemSound)
Animation completes in ≤1.5 seconds and cleans up all particle views
Subtask completions do NOT trigger confetti (only top-level tasks)
Completing a task that was ranked #1 shows an additional 'Top task done! 🏆' toast for 2s
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Create ConfettiView.swift as a SwiftUI overlay. Use a TimelineView or Canvas-based particle system: spawn 40 particles at random angles from the origin, each with random dx/dy velocity, fading opacity over 1.2s. Use @State particles: [Particle] with a Timer. Colours: [.white, Color(hex:'#FFD700'), Color(hex:'#87CEEB'), Color(hex:'#90EE90')]. Trigger from PlannerStore.completeTask() callback. For sound: NSSound(named: 'Glass')?.play() or use a bundled .aiff. Guard parentId == nil for confetti gate.

userStories.title: Settings: appearance mode, accent colour, and font size
userStories.description: Expand SettingsView with an Appearance section: Light/Dark/System mode toggle, an accent colour picker (5 presets: Crimson Red #D32F2F, Blue, Green, Purple, Default), and a font size slider (Small/Medium/Large) that scales all body text via environment(.sizeCategory). Preferences persist via UserDefaults.
acceptanceCriteria: SettingsView has an Appearance section with Light / Dark / System segmented control
Selecting Light/Dark forces NSApp.appearance = NSAppearance(named: .aqua/.darkAqua)
Accent colour picker shows 5 colour circles; selected accent is used as .tint() on the main window
Font size slider has 3 stops: Small (body=13), Medium (body=15, default), Large (body=17)
All preferences survive app restart (UserDefaults)
swift build passes
userStories.notes: Add to Preferences.swift (or TimedPreferences): `appearanceMode: String` (system/light/dark), `accentColor: String` (hex), `fontSizeCategory: String` (small/medium/large). In TimeManagerDesktopApp, observe appearanceMode and apply NSApp.appearance. Pass accentColor as Color through environmentObject. Pass a custom sizeCategory via Environment(\.sizeCategory) override or a custom EnvironmentKey for bodyFontSize. In SettingsView, add Picker for appearance, a LazyHGrid of 5 colour circles, and a Slider with 3 detents.
